speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September 6, 1995. My name is Ferne Hasson, and I am interviewing Murray Pantirer in Hillside, New Jersey, in the United States of America. And the language of the interview is English. Today is Wednesday, September 6, 1995. My name is Ferne Hasson, and I'm interviewing Murray Pantirer. We're in Hillside, New Jersey, in the United States of America. And the Engl-- the language of the interview is English. What's your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Murray Pantirer. Spell your last name.

speaker: interviewer
text: P-A-N-T-I-R-E-R. Was that your name given to you at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the-- in-- in Europe, it was Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was Pantirer the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the name from my parents and grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they call you Moshe at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe, or sometimes, they called me [NON-ENGLISH]. Because I had two names, Moshe [NON-ENGLISH] Pantirer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 70 in June.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-- right in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the town of Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- the area that you lived in in Krakow, uh--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was called the Jewish quarters. We lived on [NON-ENGLISH] 16 near the [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] all the Jewish people live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that area have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kazmielz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-A-Z-M-I-E-L-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, ah, what were your parents' names? What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lazar Pinchas. My mother's name was Bella Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's name was Lazar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lazar Pinchas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name was Bella?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bella Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had, ah, another brother who was born in 1923. And then I was the second son. Then I had two more brothers. One was born in 1927. His name was Chaim Yosef. My older brothers was Alte Hirsch. And the fourth son, my third brother, was Mordechai Yehuda. And then in '31, my sister was born, Esther [NON-ENGLISH]. In 1933, another brother was born. His name was Yisrael David Tevye. In 1935, my youngest sister, second sister, was named Chaya Rechel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, uh, did anyone else live in the house with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your grandparents live nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not have no grandparents when-- I remember. I only had, uh, a grandmother who lived by-- by an uncle of mine, by my father's brother, Avrum Pantirer in Ozarow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ozarow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-Z-O-R-W. Ozarow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there lots of family who lived around you, aunts, uncles, cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had only one uncle who lived not far from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isaac Levenstein, my mother's brother. And his wife was Sarah Levenstein. And they had two children. One was born in 1933. And one was born in 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: And growing up, where did you go to school? And--?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived not far from the Talmud Torah. It's a Hebrew school. And normal, my days would be early in the morning I will get up, help my father to distribute some milk and eggs. He had a little store. Then we will go to the synagogue to pray. And by-- we had breakfast. 9 o'clock in the morning, we will start school, Hebrew school in Talmud Torah. I was there from 9 to 12. From 12 to 1, we went home to have, uh, our major meal. It's like dinner. Then from 1 to 4, we took Polish, history, arithmetic, the regular schools. And at 4 o'clock, we only had time to grab a piece of chocolate or coffee or chocolate milk, and we went again to the same Hebrew school as we were in the morning. And from there we were from 4 to 6. 6 o'clock, I would normally get sometimes $0.05, five groszy or 10 groszy. It all depends what I would get. We would stop by in the milk store, and we would buy a sandwich. For $0.05 I used to get a roll and butter. And if I had $0.10, I used to get a piece of herring too. And then with that, we will go to a synagogue on [NON-ENGLISH] Street, that's the Belzer synagogue. And over there, we will pray and we will do our homework. Because at home it was very hard to do homework. First, the lighting was not good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in that synagogue, we will-- we had some elderly people who took a liking to our studying. That they will sometimes buy a chocolate or some candies that they will study with us. I was very religious. And I was 14 years old when the war broke out. And I strongly believe that if one day I should not put on my tefillin, that either my hand will fall off or something terrible will happen to my parents. That's how strict religious I was. Were both sides of your family religious, both your parents? Did they come from Orthodox homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you wear a kippah--

speaker: interviewee
text: A hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: A hat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We always wore a hat. There was no such thing as going without your, uh, hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- was there anything else that you wore that-- that made you look different from other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: We wore locks, uh, by our ears. And we were dressed-- I was still young, but I was dressed, uh, modest, not flashy clothes, um, mostly grey, dark grey, or black.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said earlocks, payots?

speaker: interviewee
text: Earlocks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of your brothers and your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father had a beard, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that your father had, uh, an egg and milk store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in the h-- in the house there was a special place, in the building. And from over there, customers will come in. Mostly we will carry it to the houses. We'll distribute them to the houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's what you did in the morning? You--?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what we do in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. And did your other brothers work in the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my older brother. And the other ones were still too young. And my mother helped out.

speaker: interviewer
text: The house-- did you live in a house, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, An apartment. It was about 100 tenants. [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the street like? Were the streets paved? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was cobblestones. It was a-- a street full of buildings, uh, mostly 100 tenants. Some of them were a little smaller. Some were a little larger. And in the street, every second or third building had a little synagogue. In the street where we lived mostly lived Jewish people, except the-- the super was Gentile. And, of course, at 10 o'clock at night, he will lock the door. But in our building there was a courtyard. And the courtyard we will be play in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you travel? How did your father make the deliveries?

speaker: interviewee
text: By foot. It wasn't too far away. Mostly we did everything by foot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there cars at that time? Did people own cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no cars. There were some people, maybe a few families in Krakow. But that was already in further-- more richer sections.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, uh, any of these people, if you lived in an apartment, did any of these people have a horse and a wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, We didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went to school, uh, all of the children in your school were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you meet other children from the neighborhood who weren't Jewish? Did you ever meet anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did-- really did not come across non-Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-- not as friends, but, uh, anyone in-- everyone in your neighborhood was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aye.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-- were your parents involved in any organizations related to the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not-- there was not organizations like you have it here in America. You belong to a shul, a synagogue, and to a rebbe. Our-- most of our time was spent being busy with-- you can imagine my mother had seven children. I will never forget that the 1937 she had children every second year. In 1937, she had no children. So she said, God doesn't want me to have any more children. And she did the cooking and the washing and the ironing. And-- and we made plenty holes in the-- ripped our shirts and ripped our pants. And she did all that work. So she had no time for any other things. My father, after he finished the work, he would-- loved the synagogue. And he mostly davened. And [NON-ENGLISH] 22 there was the rebbe from [NON-ENGLISH]. It was called the [NON-ENGLISH] Rebbe. And he was a composer. He composed a lot of new songs. And my father would know how to sing. He was not exactly a cantor, but like a-- a [INAUDIBLE]. And he would be-- when he-- the rebbe composed a new song, a new prayer, he would go over with his son, [NON-ENGLISH], and with my father. So that's how he was busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the rabbi's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Rebbe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, [NON-ENGLISH] was his last name. Mm-hm. And, uh, the, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the-- in home-- in the home, all the-- all the, uh, holidays were observed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Sabbath was observed. And the street, there was not a single store open on the Sabbath.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, uh, the-- every other holiday, uh, uh-- was there ever any problem when the stores were closed on these holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your family hear word of any trouble from before 1939? Did they hear anything? Did they speak to people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in '37 and '38, refugees from Germany, Jewish people, were chased out and they would come to Krakow. And they will talk about the-- certain things that happened in Germany. But there was always an excuse that the Germans chasing out the Jews who came from Poland, who were not born in Germany. That they took away jobs from the Germans or they cheat on the Germans. And all our politicking was done in the shul. Because, uh, we had no radios. Like having a radio would be like in [INAUDIBLE] Park to have a television. We had no radios. We rely on newspapers. There are a lot of Jewish papers that talk-- Mostly, like I said, in the shul there's a prayer in the afternoon called mincha. And then there's an evening prayer that's going maariv. Between mincha and maariv, mincha you must do before sunset, and maariv you must do when it's dark. So there was always a half hour, 40 minutes in between. Most people will sit at the table and learn Talmud. But when-- the politicking of the world-- that the Anschluss, Hitler with the Austria then taking over Czechoslovakia. As a kid, you didn't dare speak out. But I listened to the discussion of the younger people. Was always alert. The younger people, I'm not talking about myself, a 14-year-old. But the ones 20, 25 30 years old will say, we've got to do something. Hitler wrote

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other people will say, what do you know about the Germans? You're [NON-ENGLISH]. Your mother's milk is still on your lips. We know the Germans. We were in Krakow in WWI. We know the Germans, and the Germans will never do this thing. And finally they said, he's doing it already. He's throwing people out from-- there was a Kristallnacht. And they was burning shuls. They were throwing people out from the country.

speaker: interviewee
text: They take away their rights. So the discussion went-- the Ger-- Hitler is crazy. Hitler may want to do it. But the German people will never allow it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And finally it got to the talk, ah, he's doing it. So the other people will always say, why would they do something to us? What did we do to the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are law abiding citizens. And it was-- the commotion was that the whole shul could hear, the argument back and forth. And finally, the elderly people won the-- the argument, because they said, we have a big God, ein grosser Gott. And God will help us. The believing in God was so strong that we felt we are protected by God. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you didn't have radios, but that you read newspapers. The newspapers, what languages did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish and-- and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And,uh, did any of the newspapers say anything that, uh, made you all, uh, question what was going on? Was there a newspaper that you followed that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, there was-- there was no question about it. It was written up, uh, in Europe some terrible things. But again, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nobody was prepared. First, they said, he's not going to touch Poland. If he's going to touch Poland, France and England and America, they are going to declare war against the Germans. And Hitler will not be that crazy to start fighting with the whole world.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was, ah-- I see it now, it was wishful thinking. But on the other hand, there was no other choice. And a family like our family, we had no place to go. You needed a lot of money. To Palestine, they wouldn't let you in with seven children. And you needed a lot of money to buy those cards, just the trip alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to America we couldn't go, because there was a very small quota. We came under the Polish quota. And the Polish quota was very small. And again, the trip alone for us it was impossible. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a possibility to go east, to go to-- to Russia? Did anybody--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was before the war, and nobody wanted to undertake communism. First of all, we didn't want to be denied our religious right. Who wanted to have their children grow up and be a communist? We did not-- the feeling of the Nazi Germans yet. But we knew how bad the communists are. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there ever any people who-- you said that there were some refugees. Did these refugees ever stay in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we-- we-- we did not have room. But they stayed, uh, in our neighbor's place. And they stayed in public, ah, Jewish public housing, like in schools [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you and your friends doing? You were young before the war. You were 12, 13. What were you and your friends--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went into a guy who was making materials-- who had some materials pieces, ah, from clothing, a tailor. And he gave us the pieces. And we make it like a baseball, a ball. And we-- we kicked that ball around. We played mostly soccer. I remember just a few months before the war, we already chipped in-- everybody chipped in $0.10. And we had a regular soccer ball. Our major sport was playing soccer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else did you do with your spare time? Did you, uh-- did you, uh--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our spare time was to study. We didn't know about going to a movie, because it wasn't proper. And we didn't have the money to go to a movie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, uh--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were very happy. We played with-- played chess, dominoes. We did not have computers, but-- other toys. But, uh-- there was always something to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did all of the boys in the neighborhood always do things together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you said you were a big family, did you ever vacation? Did you ever travel to go see family who lived elsewhere, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- one summer, in Poland there was a law that if a grownup had a ticket to go to a place during the summer months when there was vacation, he could take a-- a child with him. So I would go to the train, just one summer, and somebody took me along to see my grandmother in Ozarow. That was the only time that I remember being on vacation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a ride was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was by train about six, seven hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, uh-- but as a family, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We barely made out. We were comfortable. There was a Shabbat. There was fish and there was meat. And there was, uh, dessert. And for the holidays, we used to get a new outfit. But that's about it, nothing extravagant. But we had a good life. The family was together. We ate-- even during the weekdays when we ate dinner, 12 o'clock, or Shabbat, nobody would dare stand up from the table before my father finished his [INAUDIBLE] There was respect. Yiddish and balbatish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then did you all accompany-- the-- you-- you went to services before dinner or after? Maariv--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dinner was during lunch time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: 12 and 1 o'clock. In the evening in the regular days, the weekdays, we would go to a shul to the stiebl and do my homework, rehash the Talmud that I learned during that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was with, uh, the [NON-ENGLISH] rebbe?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] stiebl.
